title: Jump Game IX
content:
You are given an integer array nums.

From any index i, you can jump to another index j under the following rules:

• Jump to index j where j > i is allowed only if nums[j] < nums[i].

• Jump to index j where j < i is allowed only if nums[j] > nums[i].

For each index i, find the maximum value in nums that can be reached by following any sequence of valid jumps starting at i.

Return an array ans where ans[i] is the maximum value reachable starting from index i.

Example 1:

Input: nums = [2,1,3]

Output: [2,2,3]

Explanation:

• For i = 0: No jump increases the value.

• For i = 1: Jump to j = 0 as nums[j] = 2 is greater than nums[i].

• For i = 2: Since nums[2] = 3 is the maximum value in nums, no jump increases the value.

Thus, ans = [2, 2, 3].

Example 2:

Input: nums = [2,3,1]

Output: [3,3,3]

Explanation:

• For i = 0: Jump forward to j = 2 as nums[j] = 1 is less than nums[i] = 2, then from i = 2 jump to j = 1 as nums[j] = 3 is greater than nums[2].

• For i = 1: Since nums[1] = 3 is the maximum value in nums, no jump increases the value.

• For i = 2: Jump to j = 1 as nums[j] = 3 is greater than nums[2] = 1.

Thus, ans = [3, 3, 3].

Constraints:

• 1 <= nums.length <= 105

• 1 <= nums[i] <= 109​​​​​​​
hints: Think of the array as a directed graph where edges represent valid jumps.
From index i, forward jumps go only to smaller values; backward jumps go only to larger values.
The maximum reachable value from i is the maximum value in the connected component reachable under these jump rules.
You can find connected ranges by looking at prefix maximums and suffix minimums, a cut happens where all values to the left are <= all values to the right.

solutions.python:
class Solution(object):
    def maxValue(self, nums):
        n = len(nums)
        # Key insight: from i you can jump forward to any j > i with nums[j] < nums[i],
        # and backward to any j < i with nums[j] > nums[i].
        # Connected components: indices where you can reach each other form segments
        # separated by 'valley bottoms' (positions that are local minima in a specific sense).
        # Actually: find the maximum value in the entire reachable set from each i.
        # Observation: all indices in the same 'segment' can reach each other.
        # A segment is a maximal range [l, r] where prefix_max[l..r] > all values outside.
        # Simpler: compute prefix max from left and suffix min from right.
        # Two indices i, j are in the same component iff they can reach each other.
        # The max reachable from i = max of its component.
        # Components: a 'cut' at position k exists if max(nums[0..k]) <= min(nums[k+1..n-1]).
        # Within each component, all values are reachable from each other.
        
        # Compute prefix_max and suffix_min
        prefix_max = [0] * n
        prefix_max[0] = nums[0]
        for i in range(1, n):
            prefix_max[i] = max(prefix_max[i-1], nums[i])
        
        suffix_min = [0] * n
        suffix_min[n-1] = nums[n-1]
        for i in range(n-2, -1, -1):
            suffix_min[i] = min(suffix_min[i+1], nums[i])
        
        # Assign component IDs
        comp = [0] * n
        comp_id = 0
        for i in range(n):
            comp[i] = comp_id
            # Cut after i if prefix_max[i] <= suffix_min[i+1]
            if i < n-1 and prefix_max[i] <= suffix_min[i+1]:
                comp_id += 1
        
        # Max value per component
        from collections import defaultdict
        comp_max = defaultdict(int)
        for i in range(n):
            comp_max[comp[i]] = max(comp_max[comp[i]], nums[i])
        
        return [comp_max[comp[i]] for i in range(n)]
solutions.explanation:
Identify connected components: a cut occurs at position i if prefix_max[0..i] ≤ suffix_min[i+1..n-1], meaning no element on the left can jump to the right or vice versa. Within each component, all values are mutually reachable, so the answer for each index is the max value in its component.

Time: O(n). Space: O(n).
